cards.front: If I were happy, I would sing.
cards.grammarNote: لَوْ (law) introduces counterfactual (unreal) conditions. Pattern: لَوْ + past tense → لَـ + past tense. The لَـ in the result clause is a marker of the counterfactual. This describes something contrary to current reality.

cards.front: If you are sad, talk to me.
cards.grammarNote: إِذَا (idhā) introduces real conditions — things that could actually happen. Pattern: إِذَا + past/present → result. The result clause can be a command (imperative): تَكَلَّمْ (talk!). إِذَا ≠ لَوْ: إِذَا is real, لَوْ is unreal.

إِذَا كُنْتَ (idhā kunta) = if you are (lit. 'if you were'). Arabic uses the past tense in conditional clauses even for present/future real conditions. اِسْتَرِحْ is the imperative of اِسْتَرَاحَ (to rest).

cards.front: If I were rich, I would travel.
cards.grammarNote: لَوْ + كَانَ (past of 'to be') is the classic counterfactual structure. غَنِيًّا is accusative predicate of كَانَ. لَسَافَرْتُ = I would have traveled. The لَـ prefix on the result clause signals the unreal condition.

cards.front: If you feel better, we will go out.
If I had known, I wouldn't have come.
cards.grammarNote: لَوْ عَلِمْتُ = if I had known. لَمَا (la-mā) = (then) not: لَـ (result marker) + مَا (not, for past negation). This is a past counterfactual — neither condition nor result happened.

cards.front: If you feel nervous, breathe deeply.
If she were here, she would be happy.
cards.grammarNote: لَوْ كَانَتْ ... لَكَانَتْ: both the condition and result use كَانَ with لَوْ/لَـ. The result clause لَكَانَتْ سَعِيدَةً = she would have been happy. Note: سَعِيدَة in spoken form (without full tanwin).

cards.front: If you are angry, don't make decisions.
cards.back: إِذَا كُنْتَ غَاضِبًا لَا تَتَّخِذْ قَرَارَات.
cards.romanized: idhā kunta ghāḍiban lā tattakhidh qarārāt.
cards.grammarNote: Negative imperative: لَا + jussive (مجزوم). لَا تَتَّخِذْ = don't take/make (jussive of يَتَّخِذُ). Real condition with إِذَا followed by a negative command.

cards.front: If I were you, I would apologize.
cards.grammarNote: لَوْ كُنْتُ مَكَانَكَ (law kuntu makānaka) = if I were in your place. مَكَانَكَ = your place (accusative). This idiom is the standard Arabic equivalent of 'if I were you'.

cards.front: Whenever you want, I am here.
cards.grammarNote: كُلَّمَا (kullamā) = whenever/every time. Related to conditional expressions, it introduces a repeated condition. It is followed by the past tense and expresses repeated events: كُلَّمَا رَأَيْتُهُ ضَحِكَ (every time I saw him, he laughed).
